ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated stir-fried okra entry. A00LC (okra) used as the closest defensible anchor for the primary ingredient; preparation modifier 'stir-fried' noted here.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side dish serving of stir-fried lady's finger is approximately 100 g, equivalent to about 6–8 whole okra pods cooked with aromatics and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Lady's Finger — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo entry for 'Stir Fried Lady's Finger' (cr_id F14080050). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for stir-fried lady's finger. Okra (raw/cooked) is a non-starchy vegetable with very low digestible carbohydrate content (3.87g/100g). GI of 20 is estimated by analogy with other low-GI non-starchy cooked vegetables (e.g., broccoli GI ~10–15, green beans GI ~15–30) as documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per 100g serving = (20 × 3.87) / 100 = 0.77.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 359 mg/100g — borderline elevated for a vegetable dish
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 359 mg/100g is borderline for hypertension management; patients should request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce to lower sodium below 300 mg/100g, and benefit from okra's mucilaginous soluble fibre (pectin) which supports blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (3.87g/100g), low sugar (2.28g/100g), and soluble fibre (2.91g/100g) with evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties of okra pectin
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried lady's finger is well-suited for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its very low carbohydrate and sugar content combined with soluble fibre (pectin) that slows glucose absorption; maintain standard portion size and avoid added sugar-based sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.0g/100g), no trans fat, dietary fibre 2.91g/100g, and plant-based antioxidants including vitamin C (~15mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Stir-fried lady's finger presents a low cardiovascular risk profile with minimal saturated fat, no trans fat, and beneficial soluble fibre; the primary caution is the borderline sodium level (359 mg/100g) which should be reduced by requesting lighter seasoning.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very low saturated fat, no trans fat, vitamin C as antioxidant; potential nightshade sensitivity if chilli is added
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Okra is anti-inflammatory-friendly due to very low saturated fat and vitamin C content; RA patients with nightshade sensitivity should confirm whether chilli or other nightshade aromatics are included in the preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried lady's finger (okra) is a popular Singaporean vegetable side dish made by stir-frying fresh okra pods with aromatics such as garlic, chilli, soy sauce, and oyster sauce in a wok over high heat until fragrant and tender-crisp. The dish has a characteristic slightly mucilaginous texture from okra's natural soluble fibre (pectin), a savoury umami flavour from the soy and oyster sauce, and a mild vegetal taste. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside rice or as part of a mixed-dish meal in hawker centres and home cooking across Singapore, Malaysia, and South Asia. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 99 kcal, 2.19g protein, 7.6g fat (predominantly from cooking oil), 3.87g carbohydrate, 2.28g sugar, 2.91g dietary fibre, and 359mg sodium. The high fibre content — particularly okra's soluble pectin — is clinically relevant for slowing glucose absorption, supporting glycaemic control in Type 2 Diabetes, and contributing to cardiovascular health. The sodium level is borderline elevated due to soy and oyster sauce and should be moderated for hypertension management. The dish is vegetarian-friendly (without oyster sauce), halal, low-calorie, and low-GI (estimated GI ~20), making it suitable for a wide range of dietary needs.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14080050) with confidence 0.95. This is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology for non-starchy cooked vegetables, clearly documented.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for stir-fried lady's finger is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB source. calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail not available in HPB Foodo for this specific prepared dish entry as of 2026. potassium_mg (~200mg/100g) and vitamin_c_mg (~15mg/100g) are estimates based on USDA FoodData Central data for cooked okra (USDA SR Legacy #11279) used as a supporting reference for these two values only; all core macros remain HPB-sourced. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible/absent in vegetable stir-fry preparation using standard cooking oils. cholesterol_mg set to 0.0: plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing cholesterol. saturated_fat_g (~1.0g/100g) is an estimate based on typical cooking oil usage for this dish category, consistent with HPB Foodo data patterns for similar stir-fried vegetable dishes. GI of 20 is an analogous estimate for a non-starchy cooked vegetable; no direct GI measurement for stir-fried lady's finger exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The 'keto' dietary tag from the input is retained as a classification note given the very low net carbohydrate content (3.87g/100g), though this is a contextual label rather than a strict clinical designation. is_vegan set to false due to oyster sauce in the standard preparation; vegetarian flag is true as the dish can be prepared without oyster sauce.
